question: Arkansas's seat belt law makes it illegal to:
choices: Drive or ride in the front seat without wearing a safety belt — and it's a primary offense
Drive unbelted, but passengers may choose
explanation: Police can stop you just for an unbelted driver or front-seat passenger (primary enforcement, since 2009). All children under 15 must be properly restrained wherever they sit.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 9 — Arkansas Seat Belt Law

question: Arkansas requires a child to ride in a child safety seat if the child is:
choices: Under 6 years old AND under 60 pounds
Under 8 years old OR under 80 pounds
explanation: Children younger than 6 who weigh less than 60 pounds must be secured in a child passenger safety seat. All children under 15 must wear appropriate restraints while the vehicle is moving.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 9 — Child Passenger Protection Act (27-34-104)

question: You are involved in a crash where someone is injured or property damage exceeds $1,000. Arkansas law requires you to:
choices: Leave quickly to avoid blocking traffic
Stop, move your vehicle off the roadway if possible, and notify the police immediately
Wait in your vehicle without moving it under any circumstances
explanation: You must stop, move the vehicle so it doesn't block traffic, and notify police immediately when there's injury, death, or damage over $1,000. You can't leave a crash with injury or death until police have talked with you — and crashes with injury, death, or over $3,000 damage must also be reported to the Office of Driver Services within 30 days.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 80-81 — Traffic Crashes

question: When stopped by a law enforcement officer, Arkansas law requires you to provide:
choices: Your driver's license, proof of insurance, and vehicle registration when requested
Nothing until you call a lawyer
Your license only if you were speeding
explanation: You must provide your driver's license, proof of insurance, and registration when an officer requests them. Tell the officer where the documents are and ask before reaching for them.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide — What To Do When Stopped by Law Enforcement

question: Under Arkansas's distracted driving laws, a driver UNDER 18:
choices: May not use a cell phone at all while driving — not even hands-free — except in an emergency
May use a phone on rural roads
explanation: Texting is illegal for ALL drivers (27-51-1501). Under 18, no cell phone use is allowed at all, including hands-free. Ages 18-20 may use hands-free only; 21+ may use a phone but it's never truly safe.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 46 — Distracted Driving Law (27-51-1601)

question: To pass the Arkansas vision test, you must have at least:
choices: 20/70 vision in at least one eye, with or without corrective lenses
explanation: Arkansas requires at least 20/70 vision in at least one eye, with or without corrective lenses. If you have a 'B' restriction, you must wear your corrective lenses while driving or you can be ticketed.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 71 — Vision

question: After a crash with injury, death, or more than $3,000 damage to one person's property, you must file a report with the Office of Driver Services within:
explanation: File the accident report within 30 days (someone may file for you if you're injured). Separately, police must be notified immediately when there's injury, death, or damage over $1,000.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 81 — Keep a Record of Crash Information

question: At a crash scene, you should move an injured person:
choices: Only if they are in immediate danger, such as a burning vehicle
Into your car to drive them to the hospital
explanation: Don't move the injured unless danger (like fire) forces it. Check breathing, apply direct pressure to bleeding, keep them warm to prevent shock, and don't give them anything to drink.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 81 — Assisting the Injured

question: Emergency flashers (hazard lights) are for:
choices: Warning others of an emergency, a broken-down vehicle, or that you're parked on the roadside
explanation: Hazards warn other drivers of an emergency or a stopped/broken-down vehicle. In fog or rain, drive with low-beam headlights instead.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 52 — Emergency Flashers

question: Arkansas law on smoking in vehicles makes it illegal to knowingly smoke tobacco in a car carrying:
explanation: The Protection from Secondhand Smoke for Children Act (§20-27-1903) bans smoking in any motor vehicle in which a child under 14 is a passenger.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 9 — Protection from Secondhand Smoke

question: Each written (knowledge) examination in Arkansas costs:
explanation: Act 1289 of 2015 requires a $5 fee per written exam, collected with the license cost — another good reason to pass on the next try!
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 1 — Introduction (Act 1289)

question: If you have epilepsy, Arkansas allows you to drive if:
choices: You are under a doctor's care and have been seizure-free for one year
explanation: Epilepsy under medical control is usually not dangerous for driving: the standard is doctor's care plus one year seizure-free.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 72 — Health (Epilepsy)
